notes: Mountain castle of the Asai clan. Scene of Oichi's tragic fate.
Mountain castle of the Rokkaku clan. One of the largest castle ruins.
One of Japan's three great mountain castles.
Built by Toyotomi Hidenaga. Seat of the Kishu Tokugawa clan.
Built by Ukita Hideie. Also called Karasu-jo (Crow Castle).
Mountain castle of the Mori clan. Seat of Mori Motonari.
Built by the Mori clan. Destroyed by the atomic bomb in 1945.
Mountain castle site of the Kono clan in Iyo province.
Timber-reconstructed castle tower. Stands beside the Hiji River.
Seat of the Nabeshima clan. The honmaru palace has been reconstructed.
Seat of the Shimazu clan. Site of the Seinan War (1877).
Earthwork fort of the Kobayashi clan.
Mountain castle of the Kakizaki clan.
Seat of the Namioka (Kitabatake) clan.
Site of the Kunohe Rebellion (1591).
Seat of the Katakura clan. Timber-reconstructed keep.
Mountain castle of the Ando clan.
Ancient provincial fortress. Northernmost ancient provincial castle.
Seat of the Mogami and Sakai clans.
Seat of the Uesugi clan. Also called Maizuru Castle.
Seat of the Tamura clan.
Mountain castle of the Ashina clan.
Mountain castle of the Kasama clan.
Also called Kame Castle for its turtle-like shape.
Mountain castle of the Sano clan.
Built by Sanada Masayuki. Its seizure triggered the Odawara Campaign.
Seat of the Sanada clan's Numata domain.
Natural fortress of the Sanada clan.
The 'Floating Castle' that withstood Toyotomi Hideyoshi's flood siege.
Mountain castle in the Hiki hills.
Residence of Hatakeyama Shigetada.
Main castle of the Chiba clan in the Sengoku period.
Castle of the Honda clan.
Mountain castle of the Oishi clan.
Coastal battery built to defend against Western ships.
Branch castle of the Hojo clan.
Built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi during the Odawara Campaign.
Built by Takeda Katsuyori. Abandoned weeks after completion.
Mountain refuge of the Takeda clan. Takeda Shingen was born here.
One of only two Western-style star forts in Japan.
Also called the Floating Castle of Suwa.
Mountain castle of the Honjo clan.
Castle of Matsudaira Tadateru, sixth son of Tokugawa Ieyasu.
Site of the succession dispute after Uesugi Kenshin's death.
Seat of the Jinbo and Sassa clans.
One of the three great mountain castles of Etchu province.
Mountain stronghold of the Ikko-ikki in Kaga province.
Built by Yuki Hideyasu. Now the site of Fukui Prefectural Government.
Mountain castle that appears to float above the sea of clouds in autumn.
Mountain castle that repelled the Asakura invasion six times.
Built by Shibata Katsuie. Remarkably well-preserved earthwork castle.
Timber-reconstructed castle. Known for Gujodori dance festival.
Mountain castle on a rocky peak above the Kiso River.
Mountain castle. Birthplace of Mori Ranmaru.
Western base of Ishida Mitsunari during the Battle of Sekigahara.
The castle where Hojo Soun rose to power.
Built by Takeda Shingen as a base in Totomi province.
Contested between the Takeda and Tokugawa clans.
'Castle of Success' where Tokugawa Ieyasu ruled for 17 years.
Built by Oda Nobunaga. Used by Tokugawa Ieyasu in the Battle of Komaki-Nagakute.
Built by Takeda Shingen as an advance base into Mikawa province.
Also called Toyohashi Castle. Seat of the Toyohashi domain.
Renovated by Todo Takatora. Seat of the Tsu domain.
Seat of the Kitabatake clan. Mountain castle and residence complex.
Built by Kitabatake Chikafusa. Seat of the Tamaru domain.
Mountain castle built by Yabuki Yamato-no-kami. Remote mountain stronghold.
Mountain castle of the Ando clan in Omi province.
Seat of Toyotomi Hidetsugu.
Built by Akechi Mitsuhide.
Seat of Miyoshi Nagayoshi.
Mountain castle that served as Miyoshi Nagayoshi's main base.
Famous for the Kishiwada Danjiri Festival.
Seat of the Yamana clan. In the 'Little Kyoto of Tajima'.
Mountain castle of the Ogino and Saito clans.
Awaji Island castle of the Atagi clan.
Seat of Toyotomi Hidenaga.
Mountain castle built by the Akiyama clan.
Seat of the Mizuno clan.
Mountain castle of the Yamana clan in Tajima.
Also called Minato-yama Castle.
Built by Furuta Shigenari.
Site of Hashiba Hideyoshi's famous water-flood siege.
Floating castle built by Kobayakawa Takakage.
Original main castle of the Kobayakawa clan.
Seat of the Ouchi clan. One of the most powerful western lords.
Seat of the Hosokawa clan in Awa province.
Mountain castle of the Ichinomiya clan.
The first seat of the Ikoma clan when they entered Sanuki province.
Island castle of the Murakami navy. Never fell to enemy forces.
Mountain castle of the Saionji clan.
Main castle of the Chosokabe clan.
Built by Hosokawa Tadaoki. Famous for its unique roof style.
Ancient earthwork fortification built to defend against Tang invasions.
Seat of the Arima clan.
Ancient mountain fortress built to defend against Tang invasions.
Built by Terazawa Hirotaka. Also called Maizuru Castle.
Ancient mountain fortress on Tsushima island.
Seat of the Goto clan. Built in the final years of the Edo period.
Final stronghold of the Shimabara Rebellion (1637-38).
Ancient mountain fortress of the Kikuchi clan.
Seat of the Kato clan in Higo province.
Built by Kuroda Nagamasa. Seat of the Nakatsu domain.
Mountain castle of the Utsu clan.
Seat of the Ota clan in Bungo province.
Mountain castle. Seat of the Saiki domain.
Built by Takahashi Mototane.
Branch castle of the Shimazu clan.
Linked-enclosure mountain castle in Osumi province.
Mountain castle of the Sata clan.
UNESCO World Heritage Site. Gusuku built by Gosamaru.
UNESCO World Heritage Site. Gusuku of Amawari.
